core_meaning: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> has two almost opposite meanings depending on context: (1) 'appropriate, suitable, just right' (the original meaning), and (2) 'sloppy, half-hearted, random, not careful' (the colloquial meaning that now dominates casual speech). In everyday conversation, Japanese people most often use it in the negative sense — doing something carelessly or without real thought.

sentences.jp: 「どこで<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる？」「<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>決<rt>き</rt></ruby>めてよ。」
sentences.translation: 'Where should we eat?' 'Just pick somewhere, I don't care.'
sentences.notes: This is the most common everyday use — <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>決<rt>き</rt></ruby>める means 'decide casually/randomly,' showing the 'without much care' nuance.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>を<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>にやっているから、いつもミスが<ruby>多<rt>おお</rt></ruby>い。
sentences.translation: He always makes a lot of mistakes because he does his work sloppily.
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> clearly carries the negative 'half-hearted, careless' meaning that is so common in modern usage.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>には<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>大<rt>おお</rt></ruby>きさの<ruby>机<rt>つくえ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>必要<rt>ひつよう</rt></ruby>だ。
sentences.translation: This room needs a desk of an appropriate size.
sentences.notes: This is the original 'suitable/appropriate' meaning — context (no action being done carelessly) makes it clear which sense is intended.

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>時期<rt>じき</rt></ruby>に、また<ruby>ご連絡<rt>ごれんらく</rt></ruby>いただければ<ruby>幸<rt>さいわ</rt></ruby>いです。
sentences.translation: We would appreciate it if you could contact us again at a suitable time.
sentences.notes: In formal or written contexts, <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> almost always means 'appropriate/suitable' — this is the safer interpretation in business or official writing.

sentences.jp: 「レポートちゃんと<ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>いた？」「<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>いといた。」
sentences.translation: 'Did you write the report properly?' 'I threw something together.'
sentences.notes: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に〜しといた is a very natural casual pattern meaning you did something without much effort or care.

dont_use: Avoid using <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> in formal speech or writing to mean 'sloppy' — in those contexts it will be read as 'appropriate,' which can cause serious misunderstandings. Similarly, don't use it to mean 'suitable' in casual conversation, as your listener will likely interpret it as 'half-hearted.' Also, don't confuse <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> with praise — telling someone their work is <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> in a workplace context is almost always an insult.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> means 'appropriate, fitting, proper' and only has the positive meaning — it carries none of the 'sloppy' nuance of <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>. If you want to say someone gave an appropriate response in a formal or written context, <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby>な<ruby>対応<rt>たいおう</rt></ruby> is much safer than <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>対応<rt>たいおう</rt></ruby>, which could be read as 'sloppy handling.'

frequency: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> is extremely common in daily speech, especially in its 'sloppy/casual' sense. The 'appropriate' sense survives mainly in formal writing and set phrases. It is one of the most frequently misunderstood words by learners who only know the dictionary gloss.
anki_hint: Remember: in casual speech, <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> almost always means 'sloppy or without much thought' — save the 'appropriate' meaning for formal/written contexts, or better yet use <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> to be safe.
